content:
# 1 Peter Chapter 4 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), page 268 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/1PE_004_page_001.jpg

1. Therefore, since Christ suffered in the flesh, arm yourselves also with the same mind, for the one who has suffered in the flesh has ceased from sin,

2. so as to live the remaining time in the flesh no longer for the lusts of men but for the will of God.

3. For the time that has passed is sufficient for having carried out the desire of the Gentiles, having walked in licentiousness, lusts, drunkenness, revelries, drinking bouts, and lawless idolatries.

4. In this they are astonished that you do not run with them into the same flood of dissipation, blaspheming —

5. who will give account to him who is ready to judge the living and the dead.

6. For to this end the gospel was preached also to the dead, that they might be judged according to men in the flesh, but might live according to God in the spirit.

7. But the end of all things has drawn near. Be sober-minded therefore and be watchful unto prayers.

8. Above all things, having fervent love among yourselves, because love covers a multitude of sins.

9. Be hospitable to one another without grumbling.

10. As each one has received a gift, minister it to one another as good stewards of the manifold grace of God.

11. If anyone speaks, let it be as the oracles of God; if anyone ministers, let it be as from the strength that God supplies, so that in all things God may be glorified through Jesus Christ, to whom belongs the glory and the dominion unto the ages of the ages. Amen.

12. Beloved, do not be astonished at the burning among you, which comes upon you for a trial, as though something strange were happening to you,

13. but insofar as you share in the sufferings of Christ, rejoice, so that also at the revelation of his glory you may rejoice with exultation.

14. If you are reproached in the name of Christ, you are blessed, because the Spirit of glory and of God rests upon you.

15. For let none of you suffer as a murderer or a thief or an evildoer or as a meddler in the affairs of others;

16. but if anyone suffers as a Christian, let him not be ashamed, but let him glorify God in this name.

17. For it is the time for judgment to begin from the house of God; and if it begins first from us, what will be the end of those who disobey the gospel of God?

18. And if the righteous one is scarcely saved, where will the ungodly and the sinner appear?

19. Therefore let those who suffer according to the will of God commit their souls to a faithful Creator in doing good.

---
## LOGOS Translation Notes

1. **"Arm yourselves with the same mind" (v.1):** The Greek ὁπλίσασθε is a military metaphor — to take up weapons or armor. Peter draws a direct link between Christ's physical suffering and the believer's mental resolve. The Sinaiticus reading follows the standard text here. The clause "has ceased from sin" (πέπαυται ἁμαρτίας) uses the perfect tense, indicating a completed state resulting from past suffering — not sinless perfection but a decisive break from sin's dominion.

2. **"The desire of the Gentiles" (v.3):** The vice catalogue — licentiousness (ἀσελγείαις), lusts (ἐπιθυμίαις), drunkenness (οἰνοφλυγίαις), revelries (κώμοις), drinking bouts (πότοις), and lawless idolatries (ἀθεμίτοις εἰδωλολατρίαις) — represents a standard Petrine indictment of pagan social life. The term ἀθεμίτοις ("lawless, abominable") intensifies the final item, marking idolatry as the root transgression.

3. **"The gospel was preached also to the dead" (v.6):** One of the most debated verses in the New Testament. The Sinaiticus text reads εὐηγγελίσθη ("was proclaimed as good news"). Three main interpretations exist: (a) Christ preached to the spiritually dead during his descent to Hades (connecting to 3:19); (b) the gospel was preached to people now dead while they were still alive; (c) "dead" is metaphorical for the spiritually dead. The formal structure of the verse — "judged according to men in flesh / live according to God in spirit" — creates a deliberate antithesis between human and divine perspectives on suffering.

4. **"The burning among you" (v.12):** The Greek πυρώσει ("burning, fiery trial") suggests that the suffering envisioned is not merely social ostracism but a severe ordeal. Peter reframes persecution not as anomaly but as participation in Christ's sufferings (v.13), with eschatological joy as its counterpart. The phrase πρὸς πειρασμόν ("for a trial/testing") echoes the refining-fire imagery of 1:7.

5. **"The Spirit of glory and of God" (v.14):** Sinaiticus reads τὸ τῆς δόξης καὶ τὸ τοῦ θεοῦ πνεῦμα. Some later manuscripts add a clause about the Spirit being blasphemed by persecutors but resting gloriously on believers. The Sinaiticus text is shorter and likely more original. The phrase echoes Isaiah 11:2 (the Spirit of the Lord resting upon the Messiah), now extended to suffering believers.

6. **"Judgment begins from the house of God" (v.17):** Peter draws on Ezekiel 9:6, where divine judgment begins at the sanctuary. The "house of God" is the Christian community (cf. 2:5, "a spiritual house"). The argument is a fortiori: if God's own people face refining judgment, the fate of the disobedient will be incomparably worse. Verse 18 reinforces this with a quotation from Proverbs 11:31 (LXX).

7. **"Commit their souls to a faithful Creator" (v.19):** The verb παρατιθέσθωσαν ("let them entrust, deposit") is a banking metaphor — placing something valuable in trusted hands. The designation "faithful Creator" (πιστῷ κτίστῃ) is unique in the New Testament and deliberately recalls Genesis: the God who made all things can be trusted to preserve those who suffer within his purposes. The phrase "in doing good" (ἐν ἀγαθοποιΐᾳ) closes the chapter on a characteristic Petrine note — suffering does not suspend the obligation to do good but rather intensifies it.

— LOGOS, AI Translator
